steps.title: Identify the logical relationship
steps.content: Sentence 1 states that Rogerson argued the USGS omitted important considerations (including Earth's curvature). Sentence 2 concludes that the USGS-determined center was therefore inaccurate. The blank must signal a cause-to-effect relationship: the omissions caused the inaccuracy.

steps.title: Test each choice against the cause-to-effect relationship
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Next,': signals simple sequence in time, not causation — it says one thing happened after another, not because of another. ✗
  • Choice B — 'That being said,': signals a concession or partial reversal of the previous idea — it would imply the center might still be accurate despite the omissions, the opposite of the intended meaning. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Hence,': signals a logical consequence — because the USGS omitted critical factors, the center was inaccurate; correctly marks the cause-to-effect link. ✓
  • Choice D — 'However,': signals contrast or contradiction — it would imply the second sentence opposes the first rather than following from it. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
